coredata.dc:description: Rational utilization of the competition among internal functional groups in chemical adsorbents for heavy metal ions can significantly enhance adsorption efficiency, while also driving the development of advanced adsorbents. This study proposes an innovative strategy employing a group competition mechanism to develop a dual-function platform for Cd(II) detection and adsorption, along with field detection applications. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) anchor the Cd(II) detection agent, 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP), onto sodium alginate chains to form an aerogel (T-NGQDs-CAA). FT-IR and XPS characterizations confirm that NGQDs facilitate a rapid Cd(II) transfer pathway through electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking. The Cd(II) detection speed of T-NGQDs-CAA is enhanced fivefold, while density functional theory analysis reveals that TMPyP exhibits a significantly stronger binding affinity toward Cd(II) compared to nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups. Combined experimental and theoretical analyses demonstrate that this rapid ion transfer pathway intensifies TMPyP’s competition for Cd(II), thereby promoting the coordination reaction between TMPyP and Cd(II). Additionally, the Cd(II) adsorption by T-NGQDs-CAA follows the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm models, exhibiting a 148.7% increase in saturation adsorption capacity. This strategy, through constructing rapid ion channels with NGQDs and leveraging the group competition mechanism, enables in-situ Cd(II) detection and removal, enhancing the understanding and application of interactions between functional groups and metal ions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational utilization of the competition among internal functional groups in chemical adsorbents for heavy metal ions can significantly enhance adsorption efficiency, while also driving the development of advanced adsorbents. This study proposes an innovative strategy employing a group competition mechanism to develop a dual-function platform for Cd(II) detection and adsorption, along with field detection applications. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) anchor the Cd(II) detection agent, 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP), onto sodium alginate chains to form an aerogel (T-NGQDs-CAA). FT-IR and XPS characterizations confirm that NGQDs facilitate a rapid Cd(II) transfer pathway through electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking. The Cd(II) detection speed of T-NGQDs-CAA is enhanced fivefold, while density functional theory analysis reveals that TMPyP exhibits a significantly stronger binding affinity toward Cd(II) compared to nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups. Combined experimental and theoretical analyses demonstrate that this rapid ion transfer pathway intensifies TMPyP’s competition for Cd(II), thereby promoting the coordination reaction between TMPyP and Cd(II). Additionally, the Cd(II) adsorption by T-NGQDs-CAA follows the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm models, exhibiting a 148.7% increase in saturation adsorption capacity. This strategy, through constructing rapid ion channels with NGQDs and leveraging the group competition mechanism, enables in-situ Cd(II) detection and removal, enhancing the understanding and application of interactions between functional groups and metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has become a pressing global environmental challenge, necessitating the development of effective detection and remediation strategies. Cadmium (Cd), an archetypal toxic heavy metal known for its exceptional persistence, poses grave risks to ecosystems and human health [1–3]. Even at trace concentrations, cadmium can inflict irreversible damage on biological systems. What is particularly alarming is that cadmium sources are not only widespread in natural environments but also prevalent across industrial processes and daily human activities [4]. Thus, devising comprehensive strategies and advanced technologies for cadmium monitoring and removal is of paramount importance.

Heavy metal ions, characterized by high charge density, strong Lewis acidity, elevated coordination numbers, pronounced polarization, and nucleophilicity, exhibit robust interactions with active sites containing nitrogen and oxygen atoms on chemical adsorbent surfaces [5–7]. Consequently, chemical adsorbents have emerged as one of the most promising technologies for mitigating heavy metal pollution. Traditional adsorbent designs have primarily focused on functionalizing surfaces to increase the number of adsorption sites or to enhance surface reactivity [8–10]. However, recent advancements in experimental methodologies and computational modeling have ushered in a paradigm shift, driving research from surface-level optimization towards molecular-level structural design. Among these developments, competitive interactions among functional groups within adsorbents for binding the same heavy metal ion have been identified as a critical bottleneck in achieving precise and efficient adsorption performance.

Adsorbent materials often contain diverse functional groups, each exhibiting distinct affinities for metal ions, resulting in competitive binding phenomena [11–13]. For example, Bofang Shi et al., through density functional theory (DFT) calculations, demonstrated that the −N = moiety in polyaniline displays the strongest chemical affinity for metal ions [14]. This preferential interaction underscores that during the adsorption process, high-affinity functional groups dominate metal binding, whereas lower-affinity groups contribute minimally to adsorption performance [15–17]. Although direct evidence linking inter-group competition to significant adsorption inefficiencies remains limited, the disorder caused by such competition likely obstructs efforts to optimize adsorption capacity and selectivity. Moreover, conventional adsorbent design strategies seldom address these competitive interactions effectively, leaving this challenge as a major obstacle in advancing adsorption technologies.

Simultaneously, significant progress has been made in leveraging the high specific surface area and abundant active sites of adsorbents to immobilize detection agents, thus enabling integrated detection and adsorption functionalities [18–20]. For instance, Yuan Hanmeng et al. developed an integrated material utilizing carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes for Cr(VI) detection and delignified wood (DW) as the adsorption carrier. By immersing DW in a CDs solution, they achieved effective adsorption coupled with highly sensitive detection of Cr(VI) [21]. However, such simple immersion or mixing approaches face substantial challenges in achieving uniform and stable loading [22]. Furthermore, these methods often result in leaching of detection agents, not only diminishing their efficacy but also introducing risks of secondary pollution [23]. Even more critically, competitive binding among functional groups in integrated detection-adsorption materials can significantly hinder the optimization of detection sensitivity and adsorption efficiency. Detection groups typically rely on specific interactions with metal ions to generate detection signals [8,24–26]. Yet, primary binding sites are often monopolized by adsorption groups, leaving detection groups less effective. Moreover, the strong adsorption by these groups may perturb the local concentration of metal ions near detection groups, thereby undermining detection accuracy and sensitivity [14,27]. Resolving these inherent conflicts represents a crucial challenge in the design of integrated detection-adsorption materials.

To overcome these limitations, transforming competitive adsorption effects from a hindrance into an advantage, while achieving synergistic optimization of detection and adsorption, remains an essential research focus. In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were employed to construct ion transport channels, leveraging group competition to enhance cadmium ion adsorption and detection performance. Sodium alginate (SA) was covalently conjugated with NGQDs via coupling reactions to form stable amide bonds, creating a robust and stable framework. NGQDs, with their negative charges and conjugated π-system, anchored the metal sensing agent 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP), enabling the fabrication of a stable integrated detection-adsorption material(T-NGQDs-CAA). TMPyP exhibited rapid competitive binding with cadmium ions, outcompeting the nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on NGQDs-SA. This competitive interaction accelerated the detection process and markedly enhanced sensitivity. Additionally, the strong coordination interactions between TMPyP and cadmium ions, combined with the abundant adsorption sites provided by SA and NGQDs, significantly improved metal adsorption performance while addressing the leaching issues commonly associated with integrated detection-adsorption materials. This study introduces a novel nanomaterial-based strategy that accelerates functional group competition for cadmium ions, achieving rapid, in-situ, and highly specific detection and removal.

All chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received. Deionized water (DIW) with a resistance of 18.2 MΩ (Smart2pure 12, Thermo Scientific) was used throughout the experiments. Zebrafish (wild-type AB strain) were purchased from Shanghai Feisi Biological Co., Ltd. Tap water, river water, and lake water were collected from South China Agricultural University.

NGQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.21 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 0.18 g (3 mmol) of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of pure water. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was purified by dialysis for three days and then freeze-dried to obtain NGQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CAA, T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of calcium alginate aerogel (CAA) involved sol–gel processes [28]. In the sol process, 3 g of sodium alginate was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred slowly until fully dissolved. Subsequently, 1 g of CaCO3 was added and stirred to obtain a fluid sol. In the gel process, 1 g of glucono delta-lactone(GDL) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, mixed rapidly and uniformly, and then the colloidal mixture was transferred to silicone ball molds and freeze-dried to obtain CAA. In the sol step of CAA synthesis, 20 mg of TMPyP was added, and freeze-dried to obtain T/CAA. T-NGQDs-CAA was synthesized in two steps: firstly, NGQDs-SA containing different levels of NGQDs (0.1–20 mg/mL) was prepared by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide/ N-hydroxysuccinimide (EDC/NHS) (0.1 M) coupling reaction between sodium alginate (SA) and NGQDs. In the second step of the synthesis process, 20 mg of TMPyP was added, followed by freeze-drying to obtain T-NGQDs-CAA.

The morphology of NGQDs and aerogels was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM Ultra 55, Zeiss, Germany) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM). UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to measure the absorbance of solutions. The composition of the materials was investigated using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (FT-IR Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher 250i, USA), and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV, Japan). The zeta potential of the materials in aqueous solutions was measured using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZSE, UK).

To evaluate interference and selectivity, fifteen common heavy metal ions were mixed at a concentration of 200 mg/L. These ions were combined with a 2.50 mg/L Cd(II) solution and separately immersed in a T-NGQDs-CAA. The aerogel was immersed in Cd(II) solutions of varying concentrations (0.01–2.50 mg/L) for 0–4 min, and the color changes were recorded. Additionally, 20 mg of TMPyP and one T/CAA pellet were added to 20 mg/L of Cd (II) solution respectively, and the color change of the mixed solution was recorded over a period of 0–20 min.

DFT calculations were conducted to better understand the adsorption mechanisms and structures of T-NGQDs-CAA for Cd, and to elucidate the accelerating and enhancing effects of NGQDs. Given the large structure of T-NGQDs-CAA, the initial structure of T-NGQDs-CAA was simplified for DFT calculations to improve computational efficiency. The detailed computational methods are provided in the supplementary materials.

Adsorption experiments were conducted in 100 mL flasks placed in an incubator shaker at 25 °C, with all experiments performed in triplicate. The initial pH of the metal ion solutions was adjusted to a range of 2.0 to 8.0 using 0.1 M NaOH or 0.1 M HCl. Subsequently, 30 mg of aerogel was immersed in 60 mL of Cd(II) and Pb(II) solutions for 24 h to ensure equilibrium adsorption at different concentrations (10–400 mg/L). The adsorption kinetics and isotherms of T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA were evaluated through batch experiments. In the adsorption experiments using real water matrices, actual water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Cd(II) and Pb(II). The concentrations of Cd(II) and Pb(II) were determined using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (AAS, Z-2700, Hitachi, Japan). The adsorption capacity was calculated as follows:
(1)

C0 (mg/L) and Ct (mg/L) represent the liquid phase concentrations of Cd (II) or Pb (II) at the initial time and at time t (min), respectively. V (mL) is the volume of the solution, and M(mg) is the weight of the aerogel.

The biotoxicity of the aerogel was investigated using zebrafish, primarily evaluating the acute toxicity of treated water. A single aerogel was immersed in a beaker containing 30 mL of deionized water for 24 h, followed by filtration. Twenty zebrafish larvae were then introduced into the beaker and cultured for 7 days. The malformation and mortality rates of the zebrafish larvae were assessed on days 1, 4, and 7. The experiment was set up with five parallel experimental groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NGQDs anchors TMPyP: Constructs the transport pathway
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method and was described in detail in the experimental methodology [29]. TEM images present NGQDs that are well dispersed with a diameter of ∼ 4–7 nm (Fig. 1 a). The FT-IR spectra of NGQDs is presented in Fig. 1b. It is observed that the C=C and C=O stretching vibrational modes were at 1696 cm−1. The absorption peak at 3443 cm−1 indicates the O-H or N-H stretching vibration, corresponding to hydroxyl or amino functional groups. 1399 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 correspond to C-N with stretching vibration and C-O with stretching vibration, respectively. The proof is that element N is successfully doped. NGQDs exhibit quantum confinement effects, as shown in Fig. S1. The UV–visible spectrum (Fig. S1a) reveals an absorption peak at ∼ 345 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions typical of quantum dots [30]. Under 365 nm excitation, NGQDs display strong fluorescence. Specifically, under exci−tation at 300–360 nm, the fluorescence emission of NGQDs gradually intensifies, whereas under excitation at 360–430 nm, the fluorescence emission gradually diminishes (Fig. S1b). This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior confirms the presence of quantum confinement effects, with emission characteristics linked to the size and electronic structure of the quantum dots [31].

The detailed synthesis procedures of T-NGQDS-CAA, T/CAA, and CAA are described in the experimental section. T/CAA and CAA were prepared to emphasize the anchoring effect of NGQDs. Scheme 1 illustrates the assembly process of T-NGQDs-CAA. In the initial step, NGQDs are covalently linked to the sodium alginate (SA) chain via amide bond formation through an EDC/NHS coupling reaction, yielding a stable complex (NGQDs-SA). Additionally, NGQDs establish a rapid Cd(II) transmission pathway facilitated by π-π stacking, electrostatic interactions, and TMPyP self-assembly.

The formation of amide bonds is verified using FT-IR spectra and XPS. The infrared absorption peaks of NGQDs-SA and T-NGQDS-CAA at 1660 cm−1 exhibit a blue shift compared to CAA and T/CAA (Fig. 1b), indicating the formation of amide bonds (O=C-N). The C=O, C=N, C–O, C–N, C–C, and C–H groups in both T/CAA and T-NGQDS-CAA are observed in the C 1 s subpeak map (Fig. 1c). The successful addition of NGQDs induces a shift in these groups for T-NGQDs-CAA a nd introduces a new peak corresponding to O=C-N groups. In the N 1 s peak segmentation map (Fig. 1d), T/CAA exhibits only weak peaks associated with pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, whereas prominent O=C-N peaks appear in T-NGQDS-CAA.

The mechanism by which NGQDs anchor TMPyP has been investigated further. NGQDs and SA carry negative charges, whereas TMPyP has a positive charge [32,33]. The Zeta potential (Fig. S2) indicates that NGQDs increase the original electronegativity of SA, thereby enhancing the electrostatic interaction with TMPyP. Additionally, NGQDs exhibit a π-conjugated framework similar to that of graphene oxide, which interacts with the aromatic structure in TMPyP through π-π stacking. Consequently, TMPyP effectively self-assembles onto NGQDs-SA, forming a stable T-NGQDs-CAA composite. A comparison of the SEM images of T-NGQDs-CAA (Fig. 1e) and T/CAA (Fig. 1f) shows a higher density of surface protrusions on T-NGQDs-CAA, which provides evidence that NGQDs facilitate the anchoring of TMPyP onto the polymer surface through aggregation.

After immersing T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA with varying NGQDs amounts in aqueous solution for 24 h, the solutions exhibited different color intensities, and the UV absorption peak intensities showed gradient changes (Fig. 2 a). The T/CAA immersion solution displayed the deepest color and highest absorbance, while the immersion solution of T-NGQDs-CAA (20:20) was nearly transparent, with absorbance close to zero, confirming that the leaching rate of TMPyP is inversely related to the amount of NGQDs. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals that T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA display peaks for S 2p and N 1 s, and the levels of C, N, and S are higher in T-NGQDs-CAA than in T/CAA (Fig. S3). This is attributed to T/CAA containing only a limited amount of TMPyP, whereas the abundant NGQDs in T-NGQDs-CAA increase the N content, further stabilizing more TMPyP and increasing the S content. The observed changes in chemical bonding and composition demonstrate the strong anchoring effect of NGQDs on TMPyP.

To analyze the characteristics of T-NGQDS-CAA, T/CAA, and CAA, comparative studies were conducted using SEM, TGA, and XRD analyses. The chemical environments of these materials vary significantly, and the TGA profile (Fig. S4) reveals differences in mass loss between 200℃ and 600℃, specifically 44.91 % for CAA, 40.60 % for T/CAA, and 38.92 % for T-NGQDS-CAA, which are attributed to variations in their chemical bonding [29]. Changes in the chemical environment induce lattice shrinkage [34], with the XRD pattern (Fig. S5) showing a peak shift to a higher angle for T-NGQDs-CAA. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, the pore structure of T-NGQDS-CAA is more regular than that of T/CAA. The addition of NGQDs may enhance the crosslinking of aerogels, gradually increasing the channel spacing within the three-dimensional interconnected network, thereby transforming it into a layered stack structure. The porosity of T-NGQDs-CAA is quantified by its BET surface area. Fig. 2e shows a Type II isotherm with an S-shaped adsorption–desorption curve, typical of mesoporous materials. The BET surface area is 201.36 m2/g, indicating a dense structure, as supported by the morphology in Fig. 2c. Fig. 2f shows the pore size distribution from the BJH model, revealing both mesoporous and macroporous characteristics in the MHCC spheres.

The sensitivity of T-NGQDs-CAA to Cd (II) was investigated using a concentration gradient series. After a 5-minute detection period, the results were photographed and retained as reference samples (Fig. 3 a). The results indicated a gradient shift in the color of T-NGQDs-CAA from red to green within the concentration range of 0.01–2.50 mg/L. After three repetitions of the experiment, the RGB parameters of the images were extracted using ImageJ [35]. Fig. 3b illustrates the R/B ratio against the concentration of Cd (II) in the solution, resulting in a well-fitted line (R2 = 0.989). The limit of detection (LOD) for Cd(II) was determined to be 0.0050 mg/L based on ten replicate measurements and the 3σ criterion. Notably, as shown in Table 1 , a comparison of the sensing performance of different materials for Cd(II) reveals that T-NGQDs-CAA exhibits excellent potential for Cd(II) rapid detection. Utilizing the fitted linear relationship, a program was developed to analyze the images and provide the detection concentration. The code is provided in the supporting literature. This program enhances the feasibility and practicality of T-NGQDs-CAA for real-world applications.

NGQDs play a crucial role in enhancing the detection performance of T-NGQDs-CAA, as effective competition is achieved only when NGQDs interact with TMPyP to form an ion channel. The experiment compared the reaction time and color change process of T-NGQDs-CAA (Fig. 3c-I), T/CAA (Fig. 3c-II), and TMPyP (Fig. 3c-III) in detecting Cd(II). To underscore the superior performance of T-NGQDs-CAA, the TMPyP content in TMPyP and T/CAA was set to 10-fold and 20-fold that of T-NGQDs-CAA, respectively. The maximum TMPyP content in T/CAA ensures that, even if TMPyP leaches, T/CAA retains some TMPyP.

As expected, the materials showed notable differences in performance. Without the anchoring effect of NGQDs, TMPyP in T/CAA was not stabilized on the material surface. As a result, TMPyP leached significantly into the solution within 5 min, causing the red color to become more pronounced. Moreover, the binding affinity of other groups within T/CAA pellets for Cd(II) is weaker than that of TMPyP, prompting Cd(II) to preferentially bind with TMPyP in solution, which causes the T/CAA solution to change color before the pellets. The color reaction rate in the T/CAA group was comparable to that of the TMPyP group, showing minimal change within the first 5 min and requiring 20 min to fully develop color. In contrast, TMPyP in the T-NGQDs-CAA group remains stably anchored on the material surface due to the anchoring effect of NGQDs, preventing leaching. Furthermore, the rapid ion channel created by NGQDs allows the TMPyP concentration in T-NGQDs-CAA to be only one-tenth of that in the TMPyP solution group, yet the color reaction completes within 4 min. This reaction rate is five times faster than in the TMPyP solution group, with higher color intensity and faster rate than in the T/CAA group. Overall, the T-NGQDs-CAA group demonstrated significant improvements in detection rate and sensitivity, exhibiting superior Cd(II) detection performance.

The primary requirement for selective detection is the measured substance’s selectivity and anti-interference capability [42]. Fifteen heavy metal ions were selected for selectivity and interference testing (Fig. 3d). In the anti-interference experiment, T-NGQDs-CAA can sensitively detect Cd (II) (concentration: 2.50 mg/L) even when the seven ions coexist in the Cd (II) solution at a high concentration (200.00 mg/L). Minimal or no color change was observed in T-NGQDs-CAA when exposed to the other seven ionic solutions. Therefore, the detection of Cd (II) by T-NGQDs-CAA demonstrates specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism: NGQDs promotes orderly group competition within T-NGQDs-CAA
ce:section.ce:para: The difference in the color change reaction between T-NGQDs-CAA and T/CAA can be attributed to the introduction of NGQDs. In the T/CAA group (Fig. 4 a), other functional groups compete for the adsorption of Cd(II) (CAA-Cd), which hinders the effective binding of TMPyP to Cd(II). As shown in Eq. (2), in the absence of NGQDs, Cd(II) initially bound by other groups within the T/CAA spheres cannot easily transfer to TMPyP, resulting in a slower and weaker color change reaction. In contrast, in the T-NGQDs-CAA group (Fig. 4b), NGQDs and TMPyP are effectively anchored on the material surface through electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking, which stabilizes the TMPyP molecules on the surface.

Although the UV–vis (Fig. S6a) and fluorescence (Fig. S6b) intensities of NGQDs decrease with increasing Cd(II) concentration, no noticeable color change is observed in the solution upon reaction with Cd(II) under visible light (Fig. S6a). Therefore, the color change observed in T-NGQDs-CAA during Cd(II) detection is not attributed to the NGQDs themselves. Nanomaterials have been widely reported for their catalytic effects, promoting reactions in complex systems. In this case, NGQDs facilitate the transfer of Cd(II) ions, providing a pathway that enhances the competitive adsorption between TMPyP and Cd(II) from both CAA-Cd and NGQDs-Cd complexes. As shown in Eq. (3), Cd(II) initially adsorbed by the alginate-based gel (CAA) rapidly transfers to TMPyP, enhancing the reaction rate between TMPyP and Cd(II) through competitive adsorption. This accelerates the color change reaction, resulting in a stronger and more rapid response.

Based on the preceding discussion, the infrared and XPS spectra of T-NGQDs-CAA, before and after Cd(II) detection, were employed to investigate the interaction mechanism. Following Cd(II) detection, T-NGQDs-CAA exhibits a narrowed and sharpened N-H and O-H stretching vibration band at 3423 cm−1 (Fig. 5 a), attributed to an increased electron density in –NH2 and –COOH groups following reaction with metal ions [43]. A new absorption peak appears at 2850 cm−1 in T-NGQDs-CAA-Cd(II), and the peak at 2927 cm−1 becomes more pronounced, possibly due to metal ion adsorption altering the chemical environment of C-H bonds in NGQDs, inducing C-H stretching vibrations of methyl or methylene groups in the alginate chain. The infrared spectrum of NGQDs-Cd in Fig. S7 confirms this fact with a peak at 2927 cm−1. Meanwhile, the C-O, C-N, or N-H stretching vibrations originally located at 1057 and 1124 cm−1 in T-NGQDs-CAA show a red shift to 1028 and 1092 cm−1, respectively, after Cd(II) coordination. The disappearance of absorption peaks at 1330 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 suggests significant coordination of cadmium ions with nitrogen- or oxygen-containing functional groups in T-NGQDs-CAA, disrupting the original vibrational modes or inducing electronic structural changes, thus eliminating these characteristic absorption peaks. Therefore, Cd(II) primarily coordinates with N- and O-containing groups, modifying their electron density and chemical surroundings [44], which leads to shifts in vibrational frequencies and corresponding infrared absorption peaks.

The XPS full spectrum of T-NGQDs-CAA-Cd (II) (Fig. S8) revealed a distinct signal from Cd (II). The Cd 3d spectrum in Fig. 5b indicates binding energies at 412.38 eV (Cd 3d3/2) and 405.58 eV (Cd 3d5/2), confirming the presence of Cd as Cd (II). Compared to the standard binding energy range of Cd (II), the binding energy of Cd 3d shifts to a higher level due to the interaction of Cd (II) with highly electronegative oxygen and nitrogen atoms, leading to a change in binding energy [45–47]. The N 1 s peak-splitting pattern of T-NGQDs-CAA-Cd (Fig. 5c) displays a new peak at 404.32 eV, representing nitrogen atoms sharing a lone electron pair to form a Cd-N coordination bond. Pyrrolic N at 399.43 eV and Pyridinic N at 398.80 eV lose some electron density through coordination with Cd (II), donating electron density to Cd (II), which results in increased binding energies (399.65 eV and 398.95 eV). According to the O-1 s spectrum of T-NGQDs-CAA-Cd (II) (Fig. 5d), the peaks of the C-O-C, C-O-H, and C=O groups shift to 532.67, 531.34, and 530.30 eV, respectively. The XPS analysis results demonstrate that Cd exhibits electrostatic adsorption and coordination with O/N functional groups in the form of Cd (II).

Through the above analysis, it can be concluded that free amino groups, carboxyl groups, and pyridine and pyrrole nitrogen on T-NGQDs-CAA donate lone pairs of electrons to Cd (II), forming stable metal complexes. To support this conclusion, DFT calculations were further used to assess the binding energy strength [44,48,49]. In the coordination of Cd (II) with pyridine and pyrrole nitrogen in NGQDs (Fig. 6 a), as well as with the amino group (Fig. 6b) and carboxyl group (Fig. 6c), the adsorption energies are −0.389 Ha, −0.337 Ha, and −0.236 Ha, respectively. The coordination adsorption energy of Cd (II) with TMPyP (Fig. 6d) is −0.535 Ha, and its absolute value is greater than that of the first three, indicating a stronger interaction between Cd (II) and TMPyP. Thus, a competitive interaction exists between TMPyP and other groups within T-NGQDs-CAA. The combined detection results indicate that NGQDs accelerate the coordination between TMPyP and Cd (II), enhancing the speed and order of internal group competition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Orderly combination of Cd (II) to optimize adsorption properties
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs strongly anchor TMPyP and generate numerous adsorption sites for T-NGQDs-CAA. Simultaneously, the organized group competition within T-NGQDs-CAA significantly enhances adsorption performance. Additionally, the comparison of Pb (II) adsorption properties between T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA further indicates enhanced adsorption properties in T-NGQDs-CAA. Solution pH has a significant impact on metal ion morphology and the surface charge of the adsorbent [44]. The optimal pH values for T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA adsorption of Cd (II) and Pb (II) were verified within a pH range of 2.0–8.0. As shown in Fig. 7 a, as the pH increases, the adsorption capacities of T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA for Cd (II) and Pb (II) rapidly increase until reaching a maximum at pH 6. According to surface charge states indicated by the Zeta potentials of T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA at different pH values (Fig. S9), the aerogel surface was found to be positively charged at pH below 5 and negatively charged at pH above 5. The strong acidic environment quickly protonates the aerogel’s functional groups, resulting in a positive charge that causes electrostatic repulsion with metal ions, thereby inhibiting coordination and chelation [50]. As pH increases, aerogel deprotonation allows free groups (–COOH, –OH, and –NH2) to interact with metal ions through chelating coordination. The initial pH of all experimental solutions in this study was precisely adjusted to 6.

To gain deeper insight into the adsorption process, an adsorption kinetics model was developed by comparing the adsorption properties of T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA (Fig. 7c: Cd (II); Fig. 7: Pb (II)), with specific parameters listed in Table S1. T-NGQDs-CAA demonstrates a higher adsorption rate and equilibrium for Cd (II) and Pb (II) than T/CAA. The adsorption kinetics curves for Cd (II) and Pb (II) differed significantly between T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA. The adsorption of Cd (II) by both aerogels increased rapidly from 0 to 180 min, reaching equilibrium values of 90.43 % and 93.58 %, respectively. Similarly, the adsorption capacity for Pb (II) increased rapidly from 0 to 240 min, reaching equilibrium at 94.86 % and 94.40 %, respectively. The equilibrium adsorption capacities for Cd (II) and Pb (II) in T-NGQDs-CAA increased by 42.49 mg·g−1 and 28.90 mg·g−1, respectively, compared with T/CAA. The rate of metal ion adsorption and equilibrium adsorption capacity depend on the number of adsorbent sites and the concentration gradient [51]. The adsorption capacity of T/CAA for Cd (II) and Pb (II) is superior to that of CAA (the kinetic model of CAA is shown in Fig. S10a, and specific data are provided in Table S1). However, due to the leaching of TMPyP and the absence of NGQDs, the number of adsorption sites in T/CAA is only slightly increased. In contrast, the more regular pore structure of T-NGQDs-CAA may also facilitate rapid ion flow. As active sites on the aerogel surface are gradually occupied by Cd (II) and Pb (II), the repulsive force at the solid–liquid interface gradually increases, thereby decreasing the adsorption rate until equilibrium is reached.

All kinetic models in this study, including pseudo-second-order models, exhibit high correlation coefficients. Therefore, the pseudo-second-order model provides a better fit for the adsorption process, indicating that the process is controlled by chemisorption, such as coordination [52]. Additionally, the intra-particle diffusion model in the illustration reveals that the adsorption process of the two heavy metals is divided into three linear stages, with specific parameters listed in Table S2. In other words, the simulated line deviates from the origin, indicating a multilinear trend, suggesting that the adsorption process is controlled by multiple steps, including external diffusion, intra-particle diffusion, and surface adsorption [53].

Adsorption isotherms provide insights into the interaction between heavy metals and aerogels and evaluate the adsorption capacity. Fig. 7e and f present the adsorption isotherm curves of Cd (II) and Pb (II), respectively. Langmuir and Freundlich models were applied to analyze the adsorption data, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table S3. The Langmuir model exhibited a higher correlation coefficient than the Freundlich model, indicating that the adsorption process of Cd (II) and Pb (II) follows homogeneous monolayer adsorption. According to the Langmuir model fitting results, the maximum adsorption capacities of T-NGQDs-CAA for Cd(II) and Pb(II) were determined to be 370.37 mg·g−1 and 352.11 mg·g−1, respectively. Compared to T/CAA, these values represent an increase of 121.35 mg·g−1 for Cd(II) and 186.82 mg·g−1 for Pb(II), while compared to CAA(Fig. S10b and Table S3), the increases were 142.06 mg·g−1 and 149.27 mg·g−1, respectively. Whether considering equilibrium or maximum adsorption capacity, it is clearly demonstrated that the adsorption capacity of T-NGQDs-CAA is improved. The maximum sorption capacities of other adsorbents are summarized in Table 2 , highlighting distinct variations among them. The results demonstrate that T-NGQDs-CAA exhibits excellent adsorption performance, confirming its potential as a promising adsorbent.

The recyclability of the adsorbent is a key indicator of enrichment capability. The adsorption–desorption results of T/CAA and T-NGQDs-CAA for Cd (II) and Pb (II) are shown in Fig. 7b. After five cycles, the removal rates of Cd (II) and Pb (II) by T-NGQDs-CAA remain at approximately 90.04 % and 88.46 %, respectively. In contrast, the adsorption efficiency of T/CAA decreased substantially to below 60 % after five cycles (Cd (II): 58.27 %, Pb (II): 40.13 %). The gradual reduction in removal rates may be attributed to the gradual depletion of functional groups on the aerogel and the incomplete release of formed metal complexes, which continue to occupy adsorption sites. The rapid decrease in T/CAA adsorption efficiency further supports that NGQDs enhance the stable adsorption performance of T-NGQDs-CAA. Additionally, the leaching of TMPyP during T/CAA adsorption is the primary reason for the decrease in adsorption rate. On one hand, TMPyP leaching reduced the number of T/CAA adsorption sites; on the other hand, TMPyP binds to metal ions in solution, thereby limiting the amount available for adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical water matrix detection and removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality and reliability of the developed aerogel in detecting and adsorbing heavy metal ions in real water environments, simulations were conducted using lake water, tap water, and river water as solvents. T-NGQDs-CAA exhibited excellent removal efficiency for Cd(II) and Pb(II) in both tap water (99.98 % and 99.75 %) and river water (99.69 % and 99.47 %), with no significant difference compared to deionized water (99.99 % and 99.99 %). However, in lake water, where competition with dissolved organic matter and other interfering ions is intensified, the capture efficiencies decreased to 96.32 % and 87.46 % respectively (Table S4). In the detection validation, the concentration range of Cd(II) added to the three water samples was from 0.5 to 2.5 mg/L. The recovery rates for Cd(II) ranged from 99.33 % to 104.00 % (Tap water: 99.33 % to 102.00 %, River water: 100.00 % to 103.00 %, Lake water: 102.40 % to 104.00 %), as shown in Table S5, with relative standard deviations between 1.08 % and 4.32 %. Overall, T-NGQDs-CAA demonstrates promising potential for effectively removing Cd(II) and Pb(II) in specific water environments, with exceptional performance in Cd(II) detection.

Zebrafish are highly sensitive to chemical substances and environmental pollutants, making them effective for toxicity and biological effect testing [70]. In this biosafety experiment, zebrafish were used as a model to evaluate the toxicity of CAA, T/CAA, and T-NGQDs-CAA, with the results and relevant data shown in Fig. 8 . During the 7-day culture period, co-culturing zebrafish with CAA and T/CAA led to distinct morphological abnormalities (Fig. 8a), including loss of balance, body inversion, spinal curvature, reduced activity, and tissue degradation. The survival rates for CAA and T/CAA were 93.00 % and 90.00 %, respectively, with the T/CAA group showing a 14.00 % abnormality rate. These developmental issues are due to structural damage during immersion, which also led to the presence of flocculent substances in the immersion solution. In contrast, T-NGQDs-CAA exhibited superior structural stability, showing no toxicity to zebrafish larvae, with a survival rate of 97.00 % and an abnormality rate of only 2.00 %. This demonstrates its excellent biocompatibility and potential for heavy metal removal from water.

NGQDs successfully anchored TMPyP through electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking effects, thereby constructing rapid ion channels and utilizing a competitive interaction mechanism to significantly enhance the detection efficiency of T-NGQDs-CAA for Cd(II), as well as enabling field-suitable detection. FT-IR, XPS, and DFT analyses confirmed that TMPyP exhibited stronger binding affinity for Cd(II). The rapid ion channel constructed by TMPyP through NGQDs effectively competes with other nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups for Cd(II) adsorption, thereby significantly improving detection efficiency. Additionally, the adsorption kinetics of Cd(II) and Pb(II) by T-NGQDs-CAA were consistent with the pseudo-second-order and Langmuir models, exhibiting increased saturated adsorption capacities by 148.7 % and 213.0 %, respectively, and maintaining stability after five cycles. The improvement in T-NGQDs-CAA adsorption efficiency is attributed to increased orderliness in the competitive interaction between NGQDs and TMPyP's internal anchoring groups. The feasibility and reliability of T-NGQDs-CAA were validated through experiments in real water samples, and its safety was demonstrated through ecological evaluation. This study successfully enhanced the efficiency of integrated adsorption-detection materials through a competitive interaction mechanism, advancing understanding and application of the interaction mechanisms between active groups and heavy metal ions.
